description: This figure presents the temperature dependency of the compressive yield stress for Fe–15Al-based alloys tested from room temperature up to 800 °C. The plot contains five distinct curves corresponding to the binary Fe-15Al matrix and four ternary variants alloyed with 2 at.% Ti, V, Nb, or Ta plus 1 at.% C, specifically Fe-15Al, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. The vertical axis denotes yield stress in compression in MPa, while the horizontal axis denotes temperature in degrees Celsius. All five alloys exhibit a gradual, monotonous decrease in yield strength as temperature increases. Notably, the Nb- and Ta-containing alloys maintain the highest yield stresses across the entire temperature range, starting near 830 MPa and 860 MPa at room temperature respectively, and remaining significantly above the Ti- and V-modified alloys and the binary Fe-15Al baseline. Above approximately 600 °C, the curves converge more rapidly toward low stress values. The caption and text confirm that precipitation strengthening via MC carbides is effective for all compositions, with NbC and TaC providing superior high-temperature stability and the greatest resistance to strength loss with increasing temperature.
